content.text: Why PoC Exit Criteria Must Be Set Before the PoC Starts

A proof of concept for a Claude integration has one purpose: to answer the technical and business feasibility questions that cannot be answered by design alone. The PoC proves (or disproves) that Claude can produce outputs of sufficient quality on the target domain, at the required latency, with data in the form it will actually exist in production.

The failure mode that turns a useful PoC into a time-consuming distraction is the absence of pre-agreed exit criteria. Without exit criteria, two things happen: the PoC never officially ends (teams keep tweaking rather than making a go/no-go decision), and scope creep accumulates (the PoC becomes a partial production build, inheriting production requirements without production rigour). Pre-agreed exit criteria prevent both problems by defining, before any code is written, exactly what the PoC must demonstrate to be declared a success and for the transition to production to begin.

Set exit criteria before the PoC starts, not after it produces results. Exit criteria decided after the results are known are not criteria — they are post-hoc rationalisations. Stakeholders who see a good PoC result will loosen any criteria that the PoC does not meet; stakeholders who see a poor result will tighten any criteria the PoC does meet. The criteria must be written when no one knows what the results will be.
The Two-Gate Model

attrs.alt: Diagram showing PoC to production pilot (exit gate) to full production (scale gate), with five checklist areas below: technical, business, security, compliance, operational

content.text: Enterprise Claude deployments use a two-gate model between PoC and full production. The exit gate approves transition from PoC to a production pilot (a limited deployment to a defined subset of users under close monitoring). The scale gate approves expansion from the pilot to full production rollout. The two gates serve different purposes:
Exit gate:

Validates that the technical approach is sound, the quality bar is achievable, and the minimum compliance and security requirements are met. The exit gate is a go/no-go decision on whether to invest in production engineering.
Scale gate:

Validates that the production pilot demonstrated the expected outcomes, that the operational model is working, and that the system is ready for the full user population. The scale gate is a go/no-go decision on full rollout.
The exit gate checklist is the subject of this document. The scale gate uses a subset of the same checklist augmented with pilot outcome data.
Technical Exit Criteria

Technical exit criteria confirm that the core Claude integration is working correctly and will work at production scale. Each criterion has an associated acceptance test that must pass before the criterion is considered met.
Criterion
What It Verifies
Acceptance Test
Who Signs Off
Quality bar achieved on pre-agreed test set
System accuracy meets or exceeds the minimum quality bar defined during discovery
Run full eval suite; score >= minimum bar on all primary quality dimensions
BU lead + system architect
P95 latency within NFR
Response time meets the latency NFR under representative load
Load test with concurrent user count from NFR; measure P95 and P99 latency
System architect
Error handling and graceful degradation
System handles API errors, timeouts, and malformed inputs without crashing or exposing internal errors to users
Simulate API unavailability; verify fallback message displayed; verify no stack traces leaked
System architect
Context window management
System stays within context limits under maximum expected input sizes
Send maximum-size inputs; verify no context overflow errors; verify token count monitoring active
System architect
Observability live
Logging, metrics, and alerting are operational before go-live
Generate test events for each alert; verify alerts fire within SLA
System architect
Scalability estimate validated
Rate limit headroom is sufficient for pilot user volume
Calculate tokens/minute at pilot volume; confirm within API tier limits with 30% headroom
System architect
Business Exit Criteria

Business exit criteria confirm that the programme has the stakeholder alignment, baseline data, and operational support model needed to run a production pilot successfully. These criteria are often neglected because they feel less tangible than technical criteria — but a technically working system deployed without stakeholder alignment, baseline data, or user training will produce poor results that are misattributed to the technology.
Criterion
What It Verifies
Evidence Required
Who Signs Off
Stakeholder sign-off on scope and success criteria
BU lead and programme sponsor have actively reviewed and approved the production scope and the north-star KPI and quality bars
Signed success criteria specification document; date of sign-off recorded
Programme sponsor
Baseline measurement complete
Pre-integration baseline data collected for all north-star and supporting metrics
Baseline measurement report with data sources, collection period, and statistical summary
Operations lead
ROI model agreed and stress-tested
Business case ROI model has been reviewed by finance; sensitivity analysis run on key assumptions
Finance sign-off on ROI model; sensitivity analysis showing breakeven at what improvement level
Finance representative
Pilot user group defined and committed
Named users or teams committed to participate in pilot and provide feedback
Signed pilot participation agreement or equivalent; contact list with named managers
BU lead
User training complete
Pilot users have completed required training on the system, its limitations, and how to escalate issues
Training completion records; assessed where training includes assessment
Change lead
Support model operational
Process for users to report issues, ask questions, and escalate is defined and staffed
Support channel created; named first-line support contacts identified; response SLA published to users
Programme lead
Security Exit Criteria

Security exit criteria confirm that the Claude integration meets the organisation's information security requirements before any production data is processed. These criteria are non-negotiable — a system that has not passed security review cannot be deployed to production regardless of how well it performs on quality metrics.
Threat model completed:

A threat model identifies the adversarial scenarios the system must be protected against: prompt injection, data exfiltration via outputs, unauthorised access, and so on. The threat model must be documented and reviewed by the information security team.
Data classification confirmed:

The classification of all data types the system will process (input and output) must be formally confirmed. If the system processes RESTRICTED or CONFIDENTIAL data, additional controls — encryption, access logging, DLP scanning — must be in place before go-live.
Access controls implemented and tested:

Authentication, authorisation, and session management must be tested. Verify that users can only access data and functionality they are permitted to access, and that access control tests include negative cases (unauthenticated requests, requests from users outside the permitted group).
Prompt injection test suite passed:

All named prompt injection test cases must produce safe outputs. The test suite should include direct injection (adversarial instructions in user input), indirect injection (adversarial instructions in retrieved documents), and role-switching attempts.
Audit logging active and verified:

All required audit events (requests, responses, escalation triggers, error events, user identifiers) must be logging to the designated audit log with the correct retention period applied. Verify by generating test events and confirming they appear in the audit log with correct fields.
Security review sign-off:

The information security team must have reviewed the threat model, data classification, access controls, prompt injection test results, and audit logging before sign-off. This review is a formal activity with a named reviewer and a written sign-off record.
Compliance Exit Criteria

Compliance exit criteria confirm that the system meets the legal and regulatory requirements applicable to the integration's data types and use case. The specific requirements depend on jurisdiction, industry, and the nature of the data processed.
Compliance Area
Criterion
Evidence Required
Data protection (GDPR / equivalent)
Data Processing Agreement with Anthropic in place; lawful basis for processing AI inputs documented; data subject rights process defined
Signed DPA; lawful basis register entry; DSAR process documented
Data retention and deletion
Retention period for AI inputs, outputs, and logs defined and configured; deletion process tested
Retention policy documented; log retention configuration verified; test deletion confirmed
Regulatory notifications
Any regulatory notifications required (AI governance committee, industry regulator, internal audit) have been filed
Notification records; acknowledgements where required
Legal review
Legal team has reviewed the system prompt, data flows, and output types for compliance with applicable law
Legal sign-off email or formal review record with named reviewer and date
Policy exceptions filed
Any exceptions to internal information security or AI use policy required for this integration have been formally approved
Policy exception approval records; named approvers; expiry dates
Operational Exit Criteria

Operational exit criteria confirm that the team responsible for running the system in production has the documentation, processes, and capabilities they need before the first production user is onboarded.
Runbook written and reviewed:

A runbook documents all operational procedures: how to start and stop the system, how to respond to each alert, how to perform common maintenance tasks, and who to escalate to for each type of incident. The runbook must be reviewed by someone other than the author before go-live.
On-call rotation assigned:

Named individuals covering the on-call rotation for the pilot period must be identified and briefed. They must have access to the runbook, the monitoring dashboard, and all credentials required to execute the runbook procedures.
Incident response process defined:

The process for responding to a production incident — from first alert to resolution — must be documented, including the escalation chain, communication templates for user notifications, and the post-incident review process.
Rollback plan tested:

If the system must be reverted (to the pre-Claude process or to a prior system prompt version), the rollback procedure must be tested before go-live. A rollback plan that has never been tested is not a rollback plan.
Cost monitoring and alerts active:

API cost monitoring must be live before go-live, with alerts configured at 80% of the monthly spend ceiling. The cost alert must route to the person with authority to take action (reduce load, switch models, or increase the ceiling).
The Exit Criteria Review Meeting

Exit criteria are not signed off asynchronously by email — they are reviewed in a structured meeting attended by all sign-off owners. The meeting has a fixed agenda: walk through each criterion in order, present the evidence for each, and record a pass or fail decision with the sign-off owner's name. Any criterion that does not pass is documented with the remediation action and owner required before a re-review.

{
  "exit_criteria_review": {
    "integration": "contract-review-assistant",
    "review_date": "2024-11-15",
    "chair": "Programme Lead",
    "attendees": [
      "BU Lead", "System Architect", "InfoSec Lead",
      "Legal Counsel", "Operations Lead", "Finance Rep"
    ],
    "outcomes": [
      {
        "area": "Technical",
        "criteria": "Quality bar achieved on 120-case eval set",
        "status": "PASS",
        "evidence": "Eval report v1.3: mean score 0.89, above minimum bar 0.75",
        "signed_off_by": "System Architect",
        "date": "2024-11-15"
      },
      {
        "area": "Technical",
        "criteria": "P95 latency < 8 seconds under 50 concurrent users",
        "status": "PASS",
        "evidence": "Load test report 2024-11-13: P95 = 5.2s, P99 = 7.1s",
        "signed_off_by": "System Architect",
        "date": "2024-11-15"
      },
      {
        "area": "Security",
        "criteria": "Prompt injection test suite passed",
        "status": "CONDITIONAL",
        "evidence": "48/50 test cases pass. 2 edge cases (indirect injection via retrieved docs) fail.",
        "signed_off_by": null,
        "remediation": "System architect to add document sanitisation layer. Re-test by 2024-11-22.",
        "remediation_owner": "System Architect",
        "remediation_deadline": "2024-11-22"
      }
    ],
    "overall_status": "CONDITIONAL — 1 criterion pending remediation",
    "pilot_go_live_target": "2024-11-25",
    "next_review": "2024-11-22"
  }
}

A conditional pass is not a pass. A conditional pass means the criterion has a named remediation with a deadline. The integration cannot go live until the remediation is confirmed and the criterion is re-reviewed. Conditional passes that are forgotten because the remediation deadline passes without a re-review are a common cause of production security incidents.
What Happens When a Criterion Fails
When a criterion fails the exit review, the team faces one of three choices. The correct choice depends on why the criterion failed and how long remediation will take:
Failure Type
Example
Appropriate Response
Criterion can be met with a short remediation (< 2 weeks)
Prompt injection test fails on 2 edge cases; fix requires adding a document sanitisation layer (3 days)
Conditional pass: document remediation action, owner, deadline. Schedule re-review. Hold pilot go-live until remediated and re-tested.
Criterion reveals a fundamental architecture problem (weeks to fix)
P95 latency is 12s under load because the retrieval architecture is synchronous; fixing requires async redesign (4 weeks)
Halt pilot: document architectural change required. Update project plan and business case. Schedule an architecture review. Do not go live until fixed.
Criterion reveals the PoC hypothesis was wrong
Quality bar is not achievable at the required latency with the chosen approach; the model that achieves the quality bar is 4x too expensive

Programme pivot or stop: return to discovery. The PoC has delivered its value by disproving the hypothesis before production investment was made. Explore alternative approaches or recommend not proceeding.

The third outcome — the PoC disproving the hypothesis — should be viewed as a success, not a failure. The purpose of the PoC was to answer the feasibility question before committing to production engineering. A PoC that reveals a fundamental problem with the approach has saved the organisation from a much larger investment in a system that would not have worked. Document the findings clearly and use them to inform the next iteration of discovery.
Automated Readiness Checks

Many technical exit criteria can be verified programmatically, reducing the manual effort required for the exit review and eliminating the risk of human error in verification. Automated readiness checks run as part of the CI/CD pipeline before any production deployment, ensuring that every deployment — not just the initial go-live — meets the technical exit criteria.

import anthropic, time, statistics

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def check_latency(system_prompt: str, test_queries: list[str], p95_target_s: float) -> dict:
    latencies = []
    for query in test_queries:
        start = time.perf_counter()
        client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query}]
        )
        latencies.append(time.perf_counter() - start)

    latencies.sort()
    p95_idx = int(len(latencies) * 0.95)
    p95 = latencies[p95_idx]

    return {
        "check": "latency",
        "p95_seconds": round(p95, 2),
        "target_seconds": p95_target_s,
        "passed": p95 <= p95_target_s,
        "samples": len(latencies),
    }

def check_context_usage(system_prompt: str, max_input: str, context_limit: int = 200000) -> dict:
    # Count tokens for worst-case input
    response = client.messages.count_tokens(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": max_input}]
    )
    usage_pct = response.input_tokens / context_limit
    return {
        "check": "context_usage",
        "input_tokens": response.input_tokens,
        "context_limit": context_limit,
        "usage_pct": round(usage_pct * 100, 1),
        "passed": usage_pct < 0.80,  # require 20% headroom
    }

def run_readiness_checks(config: dict) -> dict:
    results = []
    results.append(check_latency(
        config["system_prompt"],
        config["latency_test_queries"],
        config["p95_target_seconds"]
    ))
    results.append(check_context_usage(
        config["system_prompt"],
        config["max_expected_input"],
    ))
    overall_pass = all(r["passed"] for r in results)
    return {"overall": "PASS" if overall_pass else "FAIL", "checks": results}

Run these checks in the CI pipeline on every system prompt change and on any scheduled cadence (e.g., weekly) in production to detect environmental regressions. The checks complement — but do not replace — the human-reviewed exit criteria for security, compliance, and business readiness.
Scoping the PoC Correctly
Exit criteria are easier to write — and more likely to be met — when the PoC is scoped correctly from the start. PoC scope failures that complicate the exit criteria review:
Scope Failure
How It Manifests
Impact on Exit Criteria
PoC uses synthetic data instead of real production data
Accuracy looks acceptable on synthetic data but drops significantly on real data with noise, abbreviations, and edge cases
Quality bar criterion fails on real data after the PoC has 'passed'. Production launch delayed.
PoC excludes the security and compliance path
Team builds the happy path only; no authentication, audit logging, or data classification in the PoC
Security and compliance exit criteria require a full redesign of the PoC architecture. Timeline doubles.
PoC tests a single query type but the production system handles many
PoC demonstrates high accuracy on FAQ queries; production system must handle complaints, escalations, and edge cases that the PoC never processed
Quality bar criterion met in PoC but not in production. Exit criteria were drawn on an unrepresentative scope.
PoC runs with a developer context that users won't have
Developer tests with full knowledge of the system prompt and its limitations; real users don't know what the system can't do
User acceptance in pilot is much lower than expected. Business exit criteria pass but pilot fails.

The PoC scope definition should explicitly state which production characteristics will be replicated (data quality, query distribution, user population, security controls) and which will be simplified for speed. Any simplification becomes a risk item in the exit criteria: 'The PoC used cleaned data; production data quality validation is an exit criterion.'
Exit Criteria for Different Integration Risk Levels

Not every Claude integration requires the full five-area exit criteria review. A high-volume customer-facing integration that processes regulated data requires all five areas in full. An internal productivity tool for a small team processing only public information may require a lighter-weight review. The risk level of the integration determines the appropriate rigour of the exit criteria process.
Risk Level
Integration Characteristics
Exit Criteria Approach
High
Customer-facing; regulated data (personal, financial, health); > 1000 users; automated write actions; decisions with legal or financial consequences
Full five-area exit criteria with formal sign-off meeting, named sign-off owners for each area, and written remediation process for any failures
Medium
Internal users; business-sensitive data; 50-1000 users; read-only outputs reviewed by humans before action
Abbreviated criteria: technical and security areas in full; business, compliance, and operational areas can be verified by programme lead without formal sign-off meeting
Low
Internal users; public or non-sensitive data; < 50 users; informational outputs only; human always in the loop
Lightweight technical review: quality bar and latency checked; security self-assessment by system builder; no formal exit review meeting required

Risk level is assessed before the PoC starts, not after. The risk level determines the exit criteria template used and therefore how much effort is invested in the PoC's surrounding documentation and testing. A team that builds a high-risk integration using a lightweight exit criteria process will find that the missing criteria become production incidents.
Production Readiness Checklist: Complete Reference

The following checklist consolidates all five exit criteria areas into a single reference document. Each item should be marked as PASS, FAIL, or N/A with a brief evidence note and the sign-off owner's name. N/A items must include a rationale — 'not applicable because this integration does not process personal data' — not just a blank.
Technical Readiness
[ ] Quality bar:
Eval suite run on production-equivalent data; score >= minimum bar on all primary quality dimensions. Evidence: eval report reference. Owner: system architect.
[ ] Latency:
Load test under concurrent user count from NFR; P95 < target. Evidence: load test report. Owner: system architect.
[ ] Error handling:
API unavailability simulation; fallback message verified; no internal errors exposed. Evidence: test results. Owner: system architect.
[ ] Context window:
Maximum input tested; no context overflow; token monitoring active. Evidence: test results + monitoring dashboard. Owner: system architect.
[ ] Observability:
All alert conditions generate alerts within SLA; dashboard live. Evidence: alert test run. Owner: system architect.
[ ] Rate limit headroom:
API tier confirmed sufficient for pilot volume + 30% headroom. Evidence: rate limit calculation. Owner: system architect.
Business Readiness
[ ] Stakeholder sign-off:
Signed success criteria specification. Evidence: document reference + date. Owner: programme sponsor.
[ ] Baseline measurement:
Baseline report complete; data sources and period documented. Evidence: baseline report reference. Owner: operations lead.
[ ] ROI model:
Finance sign-off; sensitivity analysis complete. Evidence: finance approval. Owner: finance representative.
[ ] Pilot users:
Named pilot users committed; managers notified. Evidence: pilot user list. Owner: BU lead.
[ ] Training:
All pilot users completed required training. Evidence: training completion records. Owner: change lead.
[ ] Support model:
Support channel active; first-line contacts named; response SLA published. Evidence: support channel URL; contact list. Owner: programme lead.
Security Readiness
[ ] Threat model:
Documented and reviewed by InfoSec. Evidence: threat model reference + review date. Owner: InfoSec lead.
[ ] Data classification:
All data types classified; controls matched to classification. Evidence: data classification sign-off. Owner: InfoSec lead.
[ ] Access controls:
Authentication and authorisation tested; negative cases pass. Evidence: access control test results. Owner: InfoSec lead.
[ ] Prompt injection:
All named test cases produce safe outputs. Evidence: injection test report. Owner: InfoSec lead.
[ ] Audit logging:
All required events logging; retention period confirmed. Evidence: audit log sample + retention config. Owner: InfoSec lead.
[ ] Security sign-off:
Formal sign-off from InfoSec. Evidence: signed InfoSec review record. Owner: InfoSec lead.
Compliance Readiness
[ ] DPA / data processing:
Data Processing Agreement with Anthropic in place; lawful basis documented. Evidence: DPA reference; lawful basis register entry. Owner: legal counsel.
[ ] Retention / deletion:
Retention policy documented; deletion process tested. Evidence: policy document; deletion test record. Owner: legal counsel.
[ ] Regulatory notifications:
Any required notifications filed and acknowledged. Evidence: notification records. Owner: compliance officer.
[ ] Legal review:
Named legal reviewer has reviewed system prompt, data flows, and output types. Evidence: legal sign-off record with name and date. Owner: legal counsel.
[ ] Policy exceptions:
Any required policy exceptions approved with named approvers and expiry dates. Evidence: approval records. Owner: compliance officer.
Operational Readiness
[ ] Runbook:
Written and reviewed by someone other than the author. Evidence: runbook document reference + reviewer name. Owner: operations lead.
[ ] On-call rotation:
Named individuals assigned; access to runbook, dashboard, and credentials confirmed. Evidence: on-call schedule; access confirmation. Owner: operations lead.
[ ] Incident response:
Escalation chain, communication templates, and post-incident review process documented. Evidence: incident response plan reference. Owner: operations lead.
[ ] Rollback plan:
Rollback procedure documented and tested. Evidence: rollback test record. Owner: system architect.
[ ] Cost monitoring:
API cost alerts active at 80% of monthly ceiling; alert routes to named decision-maker. Evidence: alert configuration + dashboard. Owner: programme lead.
Summary

PoC exit criteria must be defined before the PoC starts, not after results are known. They have five areas: technical (quality bar, latency, error handling, observability, scale), business (stakeholder sign-off, baseline measurement, ROI model, pilot users, training, support), security (threat model, data classification, access controls, prompt injection, audit logging, security review), compliance (data protection, retention, regulatory notifications, legal review, policy exceptions), and operational (runbook, on-call, incident response, rollback plan, cost monitoring). Exit criteria are reviewed in a structured meeting with sign-off owners for each area. Conditional passes require named remediation with deadlines; the integration cannot go live until remediation is confirmed. A PoC that fails its exit criteria has succeeded in its purpose: preventing production investment in a system that is not ready.
